text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon uses a right-hand Maryland dissector to manipulate the omentum, which is covering the gallbladder.
The surgeon pushes the head backwards to separate the omentum from the lower colon and duodenum, revealing these anatomical structures within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon separates flimsy adhesions attaching the duodenum to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the fundus of the gallbladder and push it back, separating the body of the gallbladder. The duodenum is visible hanging down, with adhesions between the duodenum and Hartman's pouch being carefully dissected.
The surgeon observes that the adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding structures have thickened over time, indicating a more challenging dissection process.
The common bile duct and Hartman's pouch are not visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently pushes the duodenum downward while carefully tearing the fibrinous adhesions between the gallbladder and the duodenum.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection by the side of the gallbladder, navigating through the surgical field with visible adhesions and inflammation.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, attempting to clear the surrounding tissue and adhesions from the gallbladder area.
The surgical field reveals the anatomical line where vital structures, including the common bile duct, hepatic artery, and portal vein, are located.
The surgeon gently separates the gallbladder from surrounding structures, identifying the cystic duct, cystic artery, and bile duct during the dissection process.
The common bile duct is visible, extending across the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies potential cystic duct and cystic artery structures, carefully examining their location relative to the gallbladder.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, which may be located below the current dissection area, while carefully separating surrounding structures.
Visible pulsations are observed near the surgical site, potentially indicating the presence of a nearby artery or vessel.
Strong pulsations are visible near the gallbladder, suggesting the proximity of major vascular structures.
The surgeon identifies strong pulsations in the surgical field, indicating the presence of the hepatic artery, while noting the proximity of the common bile duct and potential cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon carefully separates tissue regions near the epiploic foramen, also known as the foramen of Winslow, ensuring safe dissection around this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon carefully identifies and separates the cleavage plane between the liver and gallbladder, avoiding the area below where the portal vein is located, to mobilize the gallbladder.
The surgeon identifies a frozen Calot's triangle, indicating dense fibrous tissue surrounding the cystic duct and artery, making dissection challenging.
The surgical field shows minimal differentiation between the common bile duct, common hepatic duct, and cystic duct, making it challenging to visually distinguish these structures.
The surgeon navigates below the common hepatic duct, approaching the area where it enters the liver, while maintaining careful dissection to avoid critical structures.
The surgeon carefully pushes tissue towards the gallbladder, avoiding pressure on the ductal structures, to facilitate the mobilization of the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the surrounding structures, including the common bile duct, common hepatic duct, and cystic duct, within the frozen Calot's triangle.
As the surgeon attempts to dissect the gallbladder, pus visibly pours out from the organ.

The surgeon uses a dissector to mobilize the inflamed and necrotic gallbladder, with pus visibly pouring out, while carefully navigating around the gallbladder to separate it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon carefully creates a cleavage between the gallbladder and the liver fossa, separating the inflamed gallbladder from the liver tissue.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers above and away from the vital ducts, ensuring they are not disturbed during the dissection of the inflamed gallbladder.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder, which is visibly distended with pus, causing some pus to pour out into the surgical field.
During dissection, blood is observed pouring out from the surgical site, which is a normal part of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, noting its abnormal position within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes pulsations on the right side, likely indicating the presence of the cystic artery or hepatic artery, visible as rhythmic movements in the tissue.
The surgeon has mobilized the cystic artery, which is visible alongside a lymph node that was previously located on the artery.
The surgeon uses a clip applicator to apply a clip on the cystic artery, which is in an abnormal position, preparing to cut it to expand the dissection area.
The surgeon directs attention to the cystic duct, preparing for further dissection or manipulation in the area surrounding the previously identified cystic artery.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is located distal to the cystic duct, with visible pulsations indicating its proximity to the hepatic artery.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, noting its orientation towards the common bile duct, which is typically directed towards the common hepatic duct.
The surgeon applies a second clip onto the cystic artery, which is in an abnormal position, and prepares to cut the artery above the clip.
The surgeon cuts the cystic artery, using a clip as a retractor. The distal side of the artery is left unclipped, and if bleeding occurs, cauterization is performed due to the proximal blood supply.
The surgeon pulls on the gallbladder to find the cleavage plane, facilitating its separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon exposes the fossa on the left side of the gallbladder, located anteriorly, revealing the anatomical space for further dissection.
The surgeon navigates beyond the gallbladder towards the liver, exploring the area between these two structures to identify anatomical boundaries.
The surgeon identifies the vertical structure below the gallbladder, likely containing the common bile duct and common hepatic duct, while the gallbladder is positioned above these ducts.
The surgeon is dissecting above the gallbladder, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the posterior Calot's triangle, encountering a thick cystic duct and numerous adhesions, which make the dissection challenging.
The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the gallbladder at the Hartmann's pouch, noting the thick and slippery tissue that makes grasping challenging.
The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the gallbladder, applying careful force due to the thick and slippery tissue.
The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to apply pressure and open the plane between the gallbladder and the liver, attempting to separate these structures above the common hepatic duct.
The surgeon continues to build up the dissection plane between the gallbladder and the liver, aiming to separate the structures and find a clear anatomical space.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully cut through tissue in a challenging area, demonstrating the technique employed in difficult cases.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the adhesions between the gallbladder and the liver, progressively finding and expanding the dissection plane between these structures.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut adhesions between the gallbladder and the liver, as blunt forceps were ineffective in building the dissection plane.

The surgeon uses scissors for sharp dissection to carefully build a plane between the gallbladder and the liver, as the gallbladder wall is tightly adhered to the liver. A stone is visible during the dissection.
The surgeon opens a section of the gallbladder, revealing pus emerging from the exposed area.
The surgeon holds the posterior wall of the opened gallbladder and cuts beyond it, navigating below the gallbladder to continue the dissection.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder towards the right side of the patient, successfully creating a plane beyond the gallbladder using sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon uses tooth forceps to grasp a portion of the gallbladder wall that remains adhered to surrounding tissue. The gallbladder wall is then cut with an opened scissor, followed by suction to clear the area.
The surgeon uses a suction tip to remove blood from the surgical field, revealing a small vessel, possibly a thickened vein or an arterial branch leading to the liver.

The surgeon uses suction to clear bleeding and pus near the body and fundus of the gallbladder, ensuring visibility during dissection. Scissors are used for sharp dissection of hard tissue, successfully separating the round fundus from the liver.
The surgeon performs a fundal-first dissection, starting at the gallbladder fundus and progressing downward towards the cystic duct, navigating through challenging anatomical conditions.

The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring the liver is protected and visible under direct vision. Some portions of the gallbladder have already been separated from the side.
The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder, revealing the thick and white cystic duct at its base.
The surgeon prepares to dissect the gallbladder downwards, indicating that clip application is not necessary at this stage.
The surgeon carefully inspects the area around the common hepatic duct, observing adhesions and ensuring that any cuts made do not involve the duct itself.
The surgeon carefully inspects the tissue from both sides to ensure it is not the duct, gently pushing it away before making an incision.
The surgeon carefully cuts through the fibrous tissue, ensuring precise dissection to reach the desired anatomical area.

The surgeon examines the area at the bottom of the gallbladder where it is attached to the common hepatic and common bile duct, noting the presence of a sessile gallbladder or a thickened cystic duct due to chronic inflammation and infection.
The surgeon uses scissors to demonstrate the common bile duct entering into the liver, highlighting its wider sessile appearance.

The surgeon carefully assesses the area around a vessel near the torn gallbladder, ensuring not to extend beyond a certain point. The surgeon plans to move slightly below the current level to ligate the vessel.

The surgeon carefully pulls the gallbladder downward to locate the area of interest, identifying the common bile duct (CBD) and surrounding fascia. The fascia is visible above the CBD and is prepared for incision.
The surgeon gently detaches fibro-fatty tissue in the area of the gallbladder, aiming to locate the cystic duct. The small fibrous tissue is removed without any visible bleeding.
The surgeon manipulates the lower part of the thick gallbladder or a wide cystic duct, maintaining control over the structure as it is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the gallbladder, achieving full separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon cautiously ligates the cystic duct, gently separating surrounding tissue little by little to ensure precise control and avoid damage.
The surgeon introduces a thread beyond the gallbladder and positions it, pulling it downwards to secure the structure.
The surgeon positions the thread around the wide cystic duct or sessile portion of the gallbladder, preparing to ligate the area with precision.
The surgeon changes the forceps to achieve a better grasp on the tissue, adjusting the instrument to improve handling during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform intracorporeal ligation on the cystic duct, positioning the thread beyond the gallbladder and pulling it downwards for secure ligation.
The surgeon positions the ligature around the thick cystic duct and sessile gallbladder, pulling it down to ensure complete encirclement and prevent leakage.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CBD) and the common hepatic duct, noting their anatomical positions as the CBD is visible below the common hepatic duct leading into the liver.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to remove tissue from the instrument, ensuring it is free to close properly. The Maryland is then used to catch the end of a thread and pull it down, sliding away to avoid entanglement in the knot. The surgeon positions the suture and applies force to tighten it securely.
The surgeon tightens a single knot, causing the gallbladder to visibly change color to blue.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CBD) and the common hepatic duct within the frozen Calot's triangle. An additional artery is visible, and the surgeon positions above the line of the duct.

The surgeon applies significant force while pulling on the tissue, causing visible jerking movements. Clips are placed on an artery, which may be an additional artery, as the cystic artery is suspected to be nearby within the tissue.
The surgeon ties a second knot, carefully catching the tip and pulling it out to secure the knot on the sector where it was previously slipped, ensuring the previous knot remains tight.
The surgeon gradually moves the suture towards the first knot, tightening it onto the thick tissue without applying excessive force, ensuring the knot securely snares the tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the common bile duct and common hepatic duct, ensuring not to injure these structures. The ducts are clearly visible, extending towards the liver, with the dissection occurring above them.
The surgeon visually inspects the ligation site to confirm that the knot is securely tightened around the tissue.

The surgeon uses scissors to leave about one centimeter of ligation, involving the sessile gallbladder, thick cystic duct, and cystic artery, all encased in fibrotic tissue, before removing the gallbladder.
